L1.area: Certificate authority request authorization and certificate lifecycle management

L2.description: An authorization bypass vulnerability exists in a certificate authority where the token reuse protection mechanism silently ignores errors from provisioner types that do not support token-based authentication. This effectively disables all authorization checks for those provisioner configurations, allowing attackers to bypass authentication entirely. Additionally, the certificate revocation endpoint does not verify that the serial number in the revocation request matches the serial number in the authentication credential, enabling unauthorized revocation of arbitrary certificates.

L3.area: ACME and SCEP provisioner token validation and certificate revocation authorization
L3.description: An authorization bypass vulnerability exists where the token reuse protection mechanism silently ignores errors from ACME and SCEP provisioners that do not support token-based authentication flows, effectively disabling all authorization checks for those provisioner configurations. Additionally, the TLS certificate revocation path does not verify that the serial number in the revocation request matches the serial number in the authentication credential, enabling unauthorized revocation of arbitrary certificates.

ground_truth.reason: The UseToken function silently ignores all errors from GetTokenID, so ACME and SCEP provisioners (which do not support token flows) have their authorization checks completely bypassed. Additionally, the revocation endpoint does not verify serial number consistency between the request and the authentication credential.
